replacement_html:
E-PRED-C (SG gravimeters 0.0 μGal): The dome predicts that superconducting gravimeters will read 0.0 μGal during a solar eclipse, claiming this confirms the eclipse magnetic signal is 'electromagnetic-aetheric in nature — not gravitational.' Three structural problems undermine this prediction.

First, the dome has no gravitational framework. A search of the dome model's entire published content reveals no gravitational mechanism — no mass model, no density distribution, no derivation of why objects fall on a flat disc. The word 'gravity' appears only in 'mag-gravity coupling' (WIN-012) and the monitoring dashboard's 'Equatorial Gravity' domain. The latter is revealing: the dome's gravity formula in monitor.py is g(r) = 9.7803 × (1 + 0.005307 × exp(−r/8619)), where 9.7803 m/s² is the WGS84 equatorial normal gravity and 0.005307 is the globe's gravity flattening factor (γpole − γequator)/γequator. The source is explicitly labeled 'WGS84 standard' in the code. The dome's gravity 'prediction' is the globe's gravity formula rewritten in dome radial coordinates. Without an independent theory of why things fall down on a flat disc, the dome cannot derive what a gravimeter should measure during an eclipse — the prediction '0.0 μGal' is asserted, not derived from any dome physics.

Second, the instruments assume globe physics. Superconducting gravimeters measure variations in gravitational acceleration (g ≈ 9.81 m/s²). They are calibrated using absolute gravimeters that measure free-fall acceleration in vacuum — a Newtonian measurement on a spherical Earth whose calibration chain runs through G, MEarth, and REarth. The dome is reading instruments whose entire calibration framework assumes the physics it claims to replace, then citing those readings as dome evidence.

Third, E-PRED-C contradicts WIN-012. WIN-012 derives the 'mag-gravity coupling constant' κ = −10.9 nT ÷ 6.5 μGal = 1.67 nT/μGal from the Mohe 1997 eclipse gravity signal. E-PRED-C predicts eclipse gravity = 0.0 μGal. If the eclipse gravity signal is zero, the denominator of κ is zero and the coupling constant is undefined — division by zero. The dome cannot simultaneously claim a 6.5 μGal eclipse gravity anomaly as the foundation of WIN-012 and a 0.0 μGal eclipse gravity reading as the confirmation of E-PRED-C. This is already flagged in WIN-012's verdict, but the contradiction runs deeper: the dome's own monitor.py hardcodes KAPPA = 1.67 as a locked constant while its predictions page asserts the measurement that would make κ undefined.

What Van Camp actually found. The dome cites 'Van Camp 1999. 0.0 μGal. Confirms electromagnetic, not gravitational.' The actual paper (Van Camp et al., 2001, J. Geodetic Soc. Japan) measured gravity at four SG stations during the August 1999 eclipse and found 'no identifiable eclipse-related change above the ambient noise.' The authors concluded this sets an upper bound on gravitational shielding (γ < 10−16 m² kg−1), consistent with standard physics predictions. Van Camp did not conclude 'electromagnetic, not gravitational' — that interpretation is the dome's addition. The null result is the standard physics prediction: the Moon's gravitational effect acts through smooth tidal forces unaffected by the optical shadow. Observing 0.0 μGal eclipse-specific signal confirms Newton, not the dome.
holes_addressed: CRITICAL: Previous text just said 'non-discriminating' — now shows three-layer structural incoherence
MAJOR: Documents that dome has NO gravitational framework anywhere in its published content
MAJOR: Traces dome's gravity formula in monitor.py to WGS84 constants (9.7803, 0.005307)
MAJOR: Van Camp citation corrected — dome claims 'confirms electromagnetic' but paper said no such thing
MODERATE: Instrument calibration chain traced through globe physics (free-fall, G, M_Earth, R_Earth)
MODERATE: WIN-012 contradiction made explicit with division-by-zero argument
new_evidence_added: monitor.py line 465: g(r) = 9.7803 × (1 + 0.005307 × exp(-r/8619)), source labeled 'WGS84 standard'
monitor.py line 214: KAPPA = 1.67 hardcoded as locked constant alongside predictions that make it undefined
Van Camp et al. 2001 actual conclusion: null result, γ < 10^-16 m²/kg, no 'electromagnetic not gravitational' claim
Comprehensive search of dome content: 'gravity' appears only 19 times across all 8 pages, never with a mechanism derivation
anticipated_objections: Dome: 'Our gravity is electromagnetic in nature, mediated by the aetheric field' → Then publish the mechanism. What equation relates the aetheric field to gravitational acceleration? How does it produce g = 9.81 m/s² at the surface? The model page offers no such derivation.

Dome: 'We use WGS84 gravity values as calibration, not as theory' → Calibration requires a theory that maps dome geometry to observables. The dome's g(r) formula IS the WGS84 formula — it's not calibrated against WGS84, it IS WGS84 with dome labels.

Dome: 'The Van Camp conclusion supports our position — no gravitational anomaly means the eclipse effect is electromagnetic' → Van Camp's null result is the standard Newtonian prediction. Tidal forces don't produce eclipse-specific gravity signals. The null result confirms standard physics, not any alternative model.

Dome: 'The WIN-012 / E-PRED-C tension is a known open problem we're working on' → Then one of them must be wrong. Either there IS an eclipse gravity signal (WIN-012) or there ISN'T (E-PRED-C). The model claims both as confirmed WINs simultaneously.
kernel_of_truth: The dome correctly identifies that solar eclipses do NOT produce measurable gravitational anomalies in superconducting gravimeters — Van Camp et al. (2001) and Sun et al. (2010) confirmed this. The dome also correctly notes that eclipse magnetic signals exist while eclipse gravity signals don't, which is a genuine observational fact. The problem is not the observation but the interpretation: the absence of eclipse gravity anomalies is the standard Newtonian prediction (tidal forces are continuous, not shadow-dependent), and the dome has no gravitational theory from which to derive ANY gravimetric prediction.

code_search.found_in: monitor.py lines 211-214 (locked constants: KAPPA=1.67), lines 464-474 (Equatorial Gravity domain: g=9.7803×(1+0.005307×exp(-r/8619)), source='WGS84 standard'), lines 477-487 (EM-Gravity Coupling: kappa_obs=10.9/6.5 hardcoded), lines 782-791 (Mascon Gravity: hardcoded 26 mGal vs 28.1 mGal)
code_search.notes: No gravitational mechanism derivation exists anywhere in the dome model. The word 'gravity' appears 19 times across all 8 dome pages: 1× home (in passing), 6× wins page (WIN-011/012/013/014 descriptions), 2× model page (mag-gravity coupling formula only), 0× predictions, 0× coordinates, 0× evolution, 0× AI context, 10× live-power (domain names and labels). Zero instances define WHY objects experience gravitational acceleration on a flat disc.

cross_reference_updates.note: WIN-012 already documents the κ=undefined contradiction with WIN-013/014. No update needed — the E-PRED-C text should cross-reference WIN-012's existing analysis.
These WINs already note the logical contradiction with WIN-011. The strengthened E-PRED-C text adds the instrument-calibration and missing-mechanism arguments. No changes needed to WIN-013/014 text.
